g.desc:
An exploded isometric view of the entire chassis. All components are shown floating apart from each other to reveal how they interconnect.
Wooden Panels: The large left and right side panels, the long bottom plate, the small front and back pieces, and the sensor holder are all separated.
Hardware: Eight screws and eight hex nuts are shown hovering near their respective holes and T-slots.
Electronics: The breadboard lid assembly is at the top, and the ultrasonic range finder is at the front, detached from its wooden holder.
The drawing uses white line art on a blue grid background, providing a clear map of every individual piece used in the build.
g.title: Disassembly Exploded View

g.desc:
A technical blueprint showing the removal of the ultrasonic range finder from the front of the chassis.
Left Detail: Labeled "Detach the Range Finder by removing Screws and Nuts." Two orange dashed arrows point downward, showing two screws being unscrewed from the bottom plate, releasing the wooden holder and sensor assembly.
Right Detail: Labeled "Remove O-Ring to detach Range Finder from Holder." It shows the rubber O-ring being unhooked from the notches on the wooden holder, allowing the rectangular circuit board with the two circular sensors to be separated.
Instruction: "Place each item on its spot on the Components sheet."
Progress Marker: In the top left corner, the number "1" is displayed next to five circles, with the first circle filled in.
Tactile & Organization Tips:
Since disassembly often leads to lost parts, these tips are important:
Hardware Recovery: As you unscrew the two screws from the front "nose" of the robot, be sure to catch the hex nuts from the T-slots. They can be small and easy to drop once the tension is released.
O-Ring Storage: The O-ring is a critical tactile part. Once unhooked, it is best to loop it around the sensor board or the wooden holder immediately so it doesn't roll away.
Component Sheet Alignment: The "Components sheet" mentioned in the text usually has 1:1 scale drawings of the parts. A student can identify the correct spot by matching the physical footprint of the ultrasonic sensor or the wooden holder to the outlines on the paper.
g.title: Disassembly Step 1

text.tspan: Place each item on its spot

g.desc:
An isometric blueprint showing the removal of the Lid (the wooden panel with the breadboard attached) from the chassis.
The Action: Two orange dashed arrows point toward the back of the robot, indicating that the Lid should be pulled horizontally out of the long notches (grooves) in the side panels.
The Mechanical Constraint: A warning icon (an exclamation point in a circle) appears at the bottom with the text: "Back piece needs to be open to slide the lid in and out." This serves as a reminder that the Back piece must be in its raised position to clear the exit path for the Lid.
Progress Marker: In the top left corner, the number "2" is displayed, and two out of five circles are filled in.
Text Prompt: "Slide Lid out of notches."
Tactile & Workspace Tips:
Clearing the Path: Before pulling, feel the Back piece at the rear of the robot. If it is flush with the side panels, it is locking the Lid in place. Slide it upward in its keyhole slots until you feel the horizontal grooves are clear.
Gripping the Lid: The white breadboard on top of the lid provides a textured, easy-to-grip surface. Use it to gently pull the wooden assembly backward.
Friction Check: If the lid feels stuck, ensure you are pulling straight back. Any twisting might cause the wooden edges to bind in the tracks.
Component Safety: Once the lid is removed, place it flat on your workspace. The breadboard contains many tiny holes and the jumper wires connected to it; keeping it flat prevents any pins from being accidentally bent or dislodged during the rest of the disassembly.
g.title: Disassembly Step 2

g.desc:
An isometric exploded blueprint showing the removal of the Right side panel.
The Action: Three orange dashed arrows point away from the side panel, indicating the removal of three screws from the exterior. Inside the chassis, three matching arrows point upward, showing the hex nuts being released from their T-shaped slots in the bottom plate.
The Joint: One final arrow at the rear shows the panel sliding away from the Back piece, disengaging the tab-and-keyhole connection.
Progress Marker: In the top left corner, the number "3" is displayed, and three out of five circles are filled in.
Text Prompt: "Detach the Right side by removing the Screws and Nuts."
Tactile & Organization Tips Catch the Nuts: As you loosen the screws on the outside, the hex nuts inside the T-slots will become loose. Use one hand to cup the inside of the panel to catch them so they don't fall into the chassis or roll off the table.
The Keyhole Release: Once the screws are out, the panel might still feel "hooked" to the Back piece. Gently slide the panel upward or the Back piece downward to align the round part of the keyhole with the tab for a smooth release.
Symmetry Check: After this step, the internal structure of the robot is fully exposed. The "skeleton" will now consist only of the Bottom plate, the Front piece, the Back piece, and the Left side panel.
Hardware Storage: You should now have 5 screws and 5 nuts total removed from the project (2 from the sensor mount in Step 1, and 3 from this panel). Placing them in a small container or on a magnetic tray is highly recommended.
g.title: Disassembly Step 3

g.desc:
An isometric blueprint showing the removal of the Back piece from the chassis frame.
The Action: Two orange dashed arrows indicate the movement. One points away from the top notch of the left side panel, and the other points out of the keyhole slot at the bottom. The Back piece is shown sliding vertically to disengage its tabs from these slots.
Progress Marker: In the top left corner, the number "4" is displayed, and four out of five circles are filled in.
Text Prompt: "Slide the Back piece off."
Tactile & Workspace Tips The "Unlock" Motion: To remove this piece, you must slide it upward first. Feel for the round part of the keyhole at the bottom of the side panels; once the tab on the Back piece reaches that circular opening, it can be pulled straight out.
Handle Identification: Remember that the Back piece is the one with the wide, oval cutout. Use this "handle" to get a firm grip while sliding it out of the slots.
Structural Shift: Once the Back piece is removed, the remaining "U" shape of the chassis (Bottom plate and Left side) will become much more flexible. Be careful not to put too much weight on the remaining side panel to avoid snapping the wooden tabs.
Component Check: By now, the only wooden pieces left attached should be the Bottom plate, the Left side panel, and the small Front piece.
g.title: Disassembly Step 4

g.desc:
An isometric exploded blueprint showing the final deconstruction of the chassis base.
The Left Side Action: Labeled with orange circles containing the number "1," three screws are shown being removed from the exterior of the Left side panel. Inside the frame, three orange arrows point upward to show the hex nuts being released from the T-slots in the bottom plate.
The Front Piece Action: Labeled with an orange circle containing the number "2," the notched Front piece is shown sliding vertically out of the two square slots in the Bottom piece.
Progress Marker: In the top left corner, the number "5" is displayed, and all five circles are filled in.
Text Prompts: "Detach the Left side by removing the Screws and Nuts" and "Slide the Front piece out of the Base".
Final Inventory & Storage Tips Completing this step returns the kit to its original state. Use these tactile checks to ensure all hardware is recovered:
Final Hardware Count: You should now have a total of 8 screws and 8 hex nuts. If your count is low, feel along the T-slots of the side panels and the bottom plate, as nuts can sometimes become wedged in the wooden cutouts.
The "Puzzle" Flat-Pack: The six primary wooden pieces (Bottom, two Sides, Front, Back, and Lid) are all flat. Stacking them by size makes them easier to store in the original kit box.
Sensor Protection: Now that the wooden holder is completely detached, ensure the ultrasonic range finder is stored in its anti-static bag or a dedicated compartment to protect the "eyes" from dust or pressure.
g.title: Disassembly Step 5

g.desc: If not already done, place each piece on its spot inthe Componets sheet
g.title: Disassembly Complete!

text.tspan: each piece on its spot in
